scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: All key entry points are present with file:line: chat_completion.rb:73, function_dispatch.rb:46, function_tool_adapter.rb:50, message_adapters/base.rb:17, transcript_adapter.rb:14, multimodal_content_adapter.rb:20, and mcp.rb:70 — the agent can pick any of these and start with targeted lookups. Minor gap: the dispatch at chat_completion.rb:186 points back to dispatch_tool_function but doesn't explicitly trace that to FunctionDispatch, leaving one hop unlinked.

specificity.rationale: Every abstraction is named with a concrete file:line and qualified module name (ChatCompletion, FunctionDispatch, MCP, MessageAdapters::Base, TranscriptAdapter, MultimodalContentAdapter); nothing is left as 'the chat code' or a vague pointer.

justification.rationale: The answer names the mixin pattern ('modules that can be mixed into Ruby classes to add AI capabilities') but never explains WHY — that the design enables a la carte composition so a host class includes only the behavior it needs; the motivation is absent, not just understated.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainties are flagged anywhere — how the mixins compose, which order they must be included, or how FunctionDispatch wires into ChatCompletion at runtime are all stated without hedging, leaving the agent to act on unverified assumptions.

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The answer provides a numbered sequence with concrete file:line for each layer — entry (chat_completion.rb:73), model call (147), tool detection (160-187), dispatch (298), transcript append (function_dispatch.rb:95-101), and recursive continuation (198-218). The round-trip is followable, but max_tool_calls handling is entirely absent, leaving the agent without a key control-flow anchor the rubric specifically requires.

specificity.rationale: chat_completion, dispatch_tool_function, FunctionDispatch, and transcript-append lines are all named with exact file:line references. However max_tool_calls and stop_tool_calls_and_respond — named items in the rubric — are nowhere cited, so an agent adding a new cutoff condition would need to find those paths independently.

justification.rationale: The answer says the recursive call continues 'until AI provides text response' but does not explain WHY — i.e. that tool-result messages are appended to the transcript (lines 95-101) so the model sees them on the next turn, closing the loop. The mechanism is named but not explained, leaving the agent to infer causality.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags no branches: max_tool_calls cutoff, stop_tool_calls_and_respond, and multiple tool calls in a single response are all unacknowledged; an agent acting on this answer would not know to verify those control-flow edges before modifying the loop.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full pipeline with file:line at every hop: declaration at function_dispatch.rb:46, @functions storage, available_tools filtering at chat_completion.rb:91-97, filtered_tools at :303-313, schema conversion in function_tool_adapter.rb:50-58 (with sub-line callouts for description/params/execute), and chat.with_tool at :382-384 — all rubric-named pieces present; an agent could add a new capability from this map alone.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named exactly — `function(name, description, **parameters, &block)`, `@functions`, `available_tools`, `filtered_tools`, `convert_tools_for_ruby_llm`, `create_tool_from_function`, `chat.with_tool` — each tied to a concrete file:line rather than vague labels.

justification.rationale: The answer lists what each step does but never explains WHY declarations are class-level (so all instances share a single tool registry), WHY a separate adapter converts them (to bridge Raix's DSL to RubyLLM's schema format), or WHY available_tools is an explicit filter (to let callers expose a capability subset); it reads as a step inventory, not a design rationale.

uncertainty.rationale: No flags about where things can go wrong: the JSON Schema parameter format, how the `required` array is populated, edge cases in `filtered_tools` (silent vs raising on unknown names), or RubyLLM tool-subclass creation details — the agent must verify these without any warning from the answer.

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three key files are mapped with file:line (transcript_adapter.rb:14-23, message_adapters/base.rb:17-47, multimodal_content_adapter.rb:20-61) and their roles in the flow are traced from transcript flattening through transform to ruby_llm_request application at chat_completion.rb:349-372; an agent could pick any adapter as an entry point with only small lookups to verify.

specificity.rationale: All three required files are cited by exact path with line ranges, methods are named (TranscriptAdapter#<<, MessageAdapters::Base#transform, MultimodalContentAdapter.translate, chat.add_message), and the role-specific branches (system/user/assistant/tool) are enumerated concretely.

justification.rationale: The answer shows what each adapter does but does not articulate why the layer exists (provider-shape normalization, Raix-to-RubyLLM contract); the cache_control path implies a why, but the overall purpose of the pipeline is implied rather than stated.

uncertainty.rationale: Conditional branches are described (Claude-3 model check for cache_control, image_url check for multimodal) but the answer does not flag these as places the agent should verify before acting; there is no explicit 'confirm before committing' signal on provider-specific or content-type-conditional paths.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full MCP flow with file:line: declaration (mcp.rb:41-56), tool fetching via client.tools (sse_client.rb:32-42, stdio_client.rb:16-22), registration into @functions via the function DSL (mcp.rb:108), and the shared dispatch loop (chat_completion.rb:180-187). The gap is that lib/raix/mcp/tool.rb is never named as its own file — 'Raix::MCP::Tool objects' appears only as an aside without pointing to the file, which the rubric calls out specifically.

specificity.rationale: Line-level citations are dense and concrete throughout (mcp.rb:99-158, sse_client.rb:47-77, stdio_client.rb:26-52, chat_completion.rb:180-187). The only concrete gap is mcp/tool.rb never appearing as a file path, leaving the agent to discover it independently.

justification.rationale: Point 6 gives a real WHY — 'Registered in @functions via function DSL… No special handling needed — existing tool loop handles both' — explaining that normalization into the same registry is the unifying mechanism. But it stops at the mechanism rather than the design intent: the model sees one uniform tool-calling contract regardless of transport, which is the deeper reason for the architecture.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer describes the differences (transport clients, schema from external server, type coercion unique to MCP path) but never flags them as 'verify before changing X' checkpoints — the agent is left to infer what might break if the transcript mirroring or parameter coercion diverges. No explicit uncertainty flags anywhere.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer enumerates all the major sites with file:line throughout — chat_completion.rb (lines 73-253, 180-187, 303-313, 382-385), function_dispatch.rb, function_tool_adapter.rb, mcp.rb with transport clients (sse_client.rb/stdio_client.rb), the adapter stack (transcript_adapter.rb, message_adapters/base.rb, multimodal_content_adapter.rb), and the full spec matrix — giving the agent a complete list it can open directly without fresh exploration.

specificity.rationale: Sites are named concretely: `dispatch_tool_function` at chat_completion.rb:184, the exact JSON shapes `{ role: 'tool', tool_call_id:, name:, content: }` at function_dispatch.rb:95-101, `filtered_tools` at 303-313, and spec line numbers per test case (e.g., nil_content_spec.rb:136-143 for `stop_tool_calls_and_respond!`); nothing is left at 'the tool code' level of vagueness.

justification.rationale: The answer organizes by structural role (array format, transcript format, definition structure, adapter, filtering) which implies why each site matters, but never explicitly states the axis — 'runs it vs declares it vs converts it vs adapts it vs tests it'; a reader can infer, but the agent must do that inference itself rather than being handed the rationale per site.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents every site as definitive with no flagging of what static reading can miss: no note that the mixin contract is only active when included in a host class, no caveat about fixtures or VCR cassettes that may encode tool-call shapes, and no warning that the MCP transport paths (SSE vs stdio) may have divergent edge cases worth verifying before committing.

map_quality.rationale: All six areas are present (chat loop, DSL, adapter, conversation adapters, MCP path, specs) with file:line throughout, so the structural skeleton is there; however, pervasive 'may need to' hedging means the agent still has to decide which edits are actually required, reducing actionability below what 'open the PR from this map alone' demands.

specificity.rationale: File names and line ranges are concrete throughout — 'chat_completion.rb:180-187', 'function_tool_adapter.rb:6-58', 'mcp.rb:108', spec files with line numbers — which is stronger than vague references; the conditional language dilutes precision but the citations themselves are grounded.

justification.rationale: Section headers ('Core Chat Loop (tool dispatch and recursion)', 'Schema Conversion (adapter layer)') do assign pipeline roles to each file, but the per-line justifications mostly restate what the code does ('update tool call dispatch loop') rather than explaining WHY that site must change to extend the contract.

uncertainty.rationale: Uncertainty is pervasive but unfocused — nearly every bullet says 'may need' without distinguishing definite changes from speculative ones; the rubric asks the agent to flag the hardest edge cases (loop re-entry logic, schema format, MCP transport shape, fixtures) so it can verify before committing, and the answer does not isolate those specific risks.
